family_history: Father: coronary artery disease, deceased age 72
No family history of epilepsy, autoimmune disease, or psychiatric illness

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a single-family home

neurological_exam.mental_status: Severely confused and disoriented to time, place, and person. Periods of marked agitation alternating with episodes of unresponsiveness lasting 20-40 seconds. Speech is severely dysarthric and disorganized with echolalia and perseveration. Unable to follow two-step commands. MMSE attempted but patient unable to cooperate; estimated score less than 10/30. Short-term memory profoundly impaired. Emotional lability with unprovoked crying episodes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Mild left-sided lower facial weakness noted. Hearing grossly intact. Gag reflex present bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline with intermittent involuntary protrusion movements. Prominent orolingual dyskinesias with repetitive lip-smacking, jaw clenching, and tongue movements observed throughout examination.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone increased bilaterally in upper and lower extremities with intermittent rigidity. Strength difficult to assess reliably due to encephalopathy and inconsistent cooperation; appears globally reduced, approximately 3/5 in all four extremities when patient engages. Intermittent dystonic posturing of both hands observed. No focal asymmetry convincingly demonstrated.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the left face, left tongue, and left upper and lower extremities. Withdraws to pain symmetrically. Detailed sensory examination limited by encephalopathy.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally, triceps 3+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 3+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Bilateral Babinski signs present (upgoing plantar responses).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to reliably perform finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin testing due to encephalopathy. Truncal ataxia observed when patient briefly sits upright with assistance.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient is bed-bound due to profound encephalopathy and quadriparesis.
neurological_exam.additional: Prominent orolingual dyskinesias with stereotyped lip-smacking, jaw clenching, and involuntary tongue movements. Episodes of autonomic instability observed during examination including transient tachycardia to 125 bpm, blood pressure fluctuations between 100/60 and 160/100 mmHg, and profuse diaphoresis. Urinary retention requiring indwelling catheter. Three brief episodes of behavioral arrest lasting 10-20 seconds each observed during the 30-minute encounter, concerning for subclinical seizures.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, weakness, abnormal movements, and behavioral changes following COVID-19 infection
patient.history_present_illness: This 50-year-old previously healthy right-handed Caucasian woman is transferred to the emergency department from an outside rehabilitation facility for worsening neurological status. Her illness began approximately three weeks ago in mid-December when she developed fever, cough, myalgia, and general malaise. She tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 by PCR and was admitted to a local hospital on December 28 after developing severe nausea, vomiting, and progressive weakness of all limbs. During that hospitalization, she became increasingly confused and exhibited disorganized behavior, including attempting to leave the ward, speaking incoherently, and displaying marked emotional lability with unprovoked crying and laughing. Notably, her oxygen saturation remained normal throughout and she showed no significant respiratory compromise. She received a course of treatment at the outside hospital including three daily pulses of intravenous methylprednisolone, sodium valproate for suspected seizure activity, enoxaparin for thromboprophylaxis, and esomeprazole. She was discharged home after brief improvement but continued to deteriorate. On January 9, she was transferred to a rehabilitation facility due to persistent emotional lability, progressive weakness affecting all four limbs, slurred speech, difficulty swallowing, urinary retention, and worsening cognitive impairment. At rehabilitation, her condition continued to decline. The physical therapy team noted she was unable to stand or walk independently and exhibited truncal ataxia. She developed new involuntary repetitive lip-smacking and jaw movements that were initially attributed to anxiety. Over the past 48 hours, her husband reports she has become nearly mute, is barely recognizable in her behavior, and has had two witnessed episodes of generalized body stiffening with rhythmic jerking of all extremities lasting one to two minutes each. She has also been noted to have episodes of profuse sweating, rapid heart rate, and blood pressure fluctuations. The patient has no prior psychiatric history, no history of autoimmune disease, no recent travel other than routine local activities before illness, and no illicit drug use.

background.overall_organization: Severely disorganized with no recognizable sleep-wake cycling
background.continuity: Continuous with intermittent periods of attenuation

findings.location: Generalized, maximal over frontocentral and central regions
findings.frequency: 1-3 Hz delta activity with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta bursts
findings.morphology: Rhythmic high-amplitude delta waves with overriding fast beta activity creating a characteristic brush-like pattern
findings.state: Present during both apparent wakefulness and drowsiness

findings.location: Right temporal onset with rapid bilateral spread
findings.frequency: Three subclinical electrographic seizures captured during 30-minute recording, each lasting 45-90 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta activity evolving to repetitive sharp-and-slow wave complexes
findings.state: Occurred during periods of behavioral arrest without overt clinical correlate

artifacts.description: Frequent movement artifact from orolingual dyskinesias and intermittent limb dystonia
Bilateral temporal muscle artifact during periods of jaw clenching

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response elicited

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating extreme delta brush pattern, generalized rhythmic delta activity, and three electrographic seizures of right temporal origin with rapid bilateral spread. The recording indicates severe diffuse cortical dysfunction with significant epileptogenic activity and high subclinical seizure burden. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 30 minutes. Frequent movement and muscle artifact from dyskinesias may obscure subtle focal features. Continuous EEG monitoring strongly recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral dentate nuclei of the cerebellum
findings.size: Left: 1.5 x 1.0 cm, Right: 1.3 x 0.8 cm
Bilateral, symmetric, approximately 1.2 cm in greatest dimension each side

findings.location: Bilateral corticospinal tracts at the level of the basal ganglia and midbrain (cerebral peduncles)

additional_observations: Resolution of previously reported diffuse cytotoxic changes from prior MRI performed during initial hospitalization
Ventricles normal in size and configuration
mri.impression: New bilateral symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions with restricted diffusion in the dentate nuclei, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and corticospinal tracts at the levels of the basal ganglia and midbrain. Prior diffuse cytotoxic changes have resolved. The distribution pattern is atypical for simple post-infectious encephalopathy and raises concern for autoimmune encephalitis or acute demyelinating process. The symmetric involvement of the dentate nuclei and corticospinal tracts in particular may be seen in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis or ADEM. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Non-specific ST-T wave flattening in leads V4-V6
No ST elevation or depression suggestive of acute ischemia
QTc mildly prolonged at upper limit
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm with non-specific ST-T wave changes. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 96 mEq/L (L), Glucose 102 mg/dL (H), CRP 50 mg/L (H), ESR 42 mm/hr (H), D-dimer 0.72 ug/mL (H), CK 245 U/L (H), SARS-CoV-2 PCR Positive (Ct value 32, low viral load)  (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in serum)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: 45 cells/uL (elevated) (4% PMN/92% lymph). Protein: 77 mg/dL. Glucose: 58 mg/dL (ratio 0.57 (serum glucose 102 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in serum). IgG index: 0.92 (elevated, normal <0.70). CSF IgG: 77.0 mg/L (elevated, normal <34.1). HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. SARS-CoV-2 PCR (CSF): Negative. TB PCR: Negative. TPHA: Negative. Bacterial culture: No growth at 48 hours. Gram stain: No organisms seen. Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:1280).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:640)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:1280)  (H).

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 2.4 x 1.8 x 1.6 cm, normal follicular pattern, no masses identified
2.1 x 1.5 x 1.4 cm, normal appearance, no masses identified
Normal size, atrophic endometrium measuring 3 mm, no fibroids or masses

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:1280)  (H).

output.impression: No evidence of occult malignancy on CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis. No ovarian teratoma, thymoma, or other neoplasm identified. Mild bilateral dependent atelectasis is likely related to prolonged bed rest. In the absence of a tumor trigger, anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in this 50-year-old patient may be idiopathic or post-infectious (following SARS-CoV-2 infection). Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, unchanged from prior LP)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 19 cmH2O. WBC: 32 cells/uL (elevated, decreased from prior 45) (3% PMN/94% lymph). Protein: 65 mg/dL. Glucose: 61 mg/dL (ratio 0.61 (serum glucose 100 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, unchanged from prior LP). IgG index: 0.84 (elevated, slightly improved from 0.92). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:640, decreased from prior 1:1280). Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells.

output.query: anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis associated with COVID-19 SARS-CoV-2

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis following SARS-CoV-2 infection: a systematic review
results.summary: Systematic review of 42 cases of anti-NMDAR encephalitis temporally associated with COVID-19. The majority of cases occurred 2-6 weeks after SARS-CoV-2 infection. Molecular mimicry between the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and NMDA receptor subunits has been proposed as a mechanism. Outcomes were generally favorable with standard immunotherapy.

results.title: Autoimmune encephalitis as a parainfectious complication of COVID-19
results.summary: Case series of 8 patients who developed autoimmune encephalitis following COVID-19, including 3 with anti-NMDAR antibodies. All patients responded to immunotherapy. The authors propose that SARS-CoV-2 may trigger autoimmunity through immune dysregulation and molecular mimicry.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in older adults: clinical features and outcomes
results.summary: Analysis of 577 patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis. Patients over 45 years had lower rates of ovarian teratoma (14% vs 52% in younger women), higher rates of non-teratoma triggers, and similar overall outcomes with appropriate immunotherapy. Delayed diagnosis was more common in older patients.

output.summary: Recent literature supports a growing association between SARS-CoV-2 infection and subsequent development of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, likely through molecular mimicry and post-infectious immune dysregulation. In older female patients, ovarian teratoma is less frequently identified as a trigger. Standard immunotherapy protocols (IV steroids, IVIG, plasma exchange, rituximab) remain effective regardless of the triggering mechanism.

output.proposed: Sodium valproate with concurrent methylprednisolone and IVIG
interactions: Valproate + methylprednisolone: Corticosteroids may decrease valproate serum levels through hepatic enzyme induction. Monitor valproate levels and adjust dose as needed.
Valproate + IVIG: No significant pharmacokinetic interaction. IVIG does not affect valproate metabolism.
warnings: Valproate may cause hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests periodically
Valproate is teratogenic; confirm pregnancy status before starting (although patient is 50 years old)
High-dose methylprednisolone may cause hyperglycemia, gastric ulceration, and psychiatric symptoms (agitation, insomnia) that may confound assessment of encephalitis symptoms
IVIG carries risk of aseptic meningitis, which may confound CSF monitoring
alternatives: Levetiracetam as alternative anti-seizure medication with fewer drug interactions
Plasma exchange (PLEX) as alternative to IVIG for first-line immunotherapy

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum osmolality 264 mOsm/kg (L), Urine sodium 52 mEq/L (H).

differential.diagnosis: Viral encephalitis (HSV or SARS-CoV-2 direct CNS invasion)
differential.key_distinguishing: All viral PCRs negative in CSF including SARS-CoV-2. HSV typically causes unilateral temporal hemorrhagic lesions. The MRI pattern of bilateral dentate nuclei and corticospinal tract involvement is atypical for viral encephalitis.

Post-infectious multifocal demyelination is consistent with ADEM, and the initial outside hospital considered this diagnosis. However, the markedly elevated anti-NMDAR antibody titer (1:1280) confirms the specific autoimmune etiology. ADEM typically responds fully to steroids and does not feature orolingual dyskinesias or extreme delta brush on EEG.

The behavioral changes and emotional lability could initially suggest a psychiatric presentation, but seizures, orolingual dyskinesias, autonomic instability, CSF pleocytosis, and abnormal MRI all point to an organic etiology.

MS more commonly involves the calloso-septal interface and periventricular white matter. The symmetric bilateral brainstem and cerebellar peduncle pattern seen here is atypical for MS. Additionally, the acute severe encephalopathy is not typical of MS relapses.
Requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies and typically thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO antibodies.
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows no occult malignancy. The specific anti-NMDAR antibody confirms the diagnosis. Other paraneoplastic antibodies (if tested) would be negative.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: secure IV access, continuous cardiac and oxygen monitoring, establish seizure precautions. Administer IV lorazepam 2 mg if active seizure.
Obtain urgent labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, CK, liver function, thyroid function, urine drug screen, D-dimer, procalcitonin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia suggesting SIADH, elevated CRP and CK, normal thyroid function, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in dentate nuclei, cerebellar peduncles, and corticospinal tracts

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis: cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs (including HSV and SARS-CoV-2), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, elevated IgG, oligoclonal bands, negative viral PCRs, positive anti-NMDAR antibodies at high titer

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage until HSV encephalitis is excluded
Extreme delta brush pattern, subclinical electrographic seizures, generalized slowing

optimal_actions.action: Optimize anti-seizure medication (continue sodium valproate; consider adding or switching to levetiracetam 1000 mg IV q12h given drug interaction profile)
Initiate first-line immunotherapy: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days (or plasma exchange 5 sessions over 10 days)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in antibody-mediated neuronal injury; clinical improvement expected over days to weeks

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to screen for ovarian teratoma or other occult neoplasm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No teratoma or occult malignancy identified in this older patient

optimal_actions.action: Manage SIADH with fluid restriction (1.0-1.5 L/day) and serial sodium monitoring every 6-8 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Gradual sodium correction without exceeding 8 mEq/L per 24 hours

optimal_actions.action: Admit to ICU for close monitoring of autonomic instability, seizure burden, and airway protection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Safe monitoring environment for fluctuating autonomic function and consciousness

optimal_actions.action: If insufficient clinical improvement after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 doses

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV steroids and IVIG or plasma exchange) once diagnosis confirmed or strongly suspected
Screen for ovarian teratoma and occult neoplasm with pelvic imaging and CT body
Optimize anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to assess subclinical seizure burden
Admit to ICU for monitoring of autonomic instability and airway protection
Manage hyponatremia from SIADH with careful fluid restriction
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder or attributing all symptoms to COVID-19 encephalopathy without excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Administering high-dose antipsychotics, which can worsen dyskinesias and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results if clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy, which is unnecessary when anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive
Rapid sodium correction exceeding 8 mEq/L in 24 hours, risking osmotic demyelination syndrome
Attributing neurological decline solely to SARS-CoV-2 without investigating autoimmune etiologies
Discharging patient without definitive diagnosis given progressive neurological deterioration
key_reasoning_points: The temporal sequence of SARS-CoV-2 infection followed by progressive neuropsychiatric decline with seizures, orolingual dyskinesias, and autonomic instability should raise immediate concern for post-infectious autoimmune encephalitis, specifically anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Extreme delta brush on EEG is highly characteristic (near-pathognomonic) of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and is not seen in other encephalitides or COVID-19 encephalopathy
The negative CSF viral PCRs (including SARS-CoV-2) rule out direct CNS viral infection, redirecting the differential toward autoimmune mechanisms
Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies at a titer of 1:1280 are markedly elevated and diagnostic, consistent with the severe clinical phenotype
ADEM was reasonably considered given the post-infectious context and MRI pattern, but the specific antibody confirmation distinguishes anti-NMDAR encephalitis from ADEM
In older patients (over 45), ovarian teratoma is less commonly identified as a trigger (14% vs 52% in younger women), but screening is still mandatory
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a common complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and requires careful management to avoid osmotic demyelination

The initial partial response to methylprednisolone pulses at the outside hospital, followed by relapse and new MRI lesions, is consistent with incompletely treated autoimmune encephalitis requiring escalated immunotherapy

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic anti-NMDAR encephalitis phenotype including progressive neuropsychiatric decline, orolingual dyskinesias, seizures, autonomic instability, extreme delta brush on EEG, and markedly positive CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies. The post-COVID-19 context adds clinical richness but the diagnostic findings clearly support the diagnosis with minimal ambiguity.
